title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
What is Statistics?
The science of collecting, organizing, analyzing, and interpreting data to make decisions under uncertainty.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
1.1 Foundations

Population — the full set of individuals of interest
Sample — a subset of the population we observe
Parameter — a numerical summary of the population
Statistic — a numerical summary of the sample

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
1.2 Types of Data

Categorical — qualitative labels (e.g. gender, colour)
Discrete — countable values (e.g. number of defects)
Continuous — measured on a scale (e.g. height, temperature)

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
1.3 Inferential Statistics
We use a sample to make conclusions about a population.
\[
\text{Sample} \xrightarrow{\text{inference}} \text{Population}
\]
Key tools: estimation, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
Key Takeaways

Statistics bridges data and decisions
Know whether your data is categorical or numerical
Inference always involves uncertainty — quantify it

title: STA258
text:
Roadmap


Core Concepts

1.1 Foundations
Population vs Sample
Unit, Observation, Variable


Main Distinctions

Parameter vs Statistic
Types of Data
Descriptive vs Inferential Statistics



This roadmap works as a navigation slide — bullet points link to later slides.

title: STA258
text:
1.1 Foundations

Definition 1.1: Statistics Statistics is the science of collecting, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, and interpreting data.


Statistics helps researchers use data to support conclusions about a larger group.


Main idea: Statistics helps us move from data to evidence, and from evidence to decisions.


Emphasize that statistics is not just formulas — it is about learning from data when there is uncertainty.

title: STA258
text:
Population and Sample



Population The entire group of individuals, items, or measurements we want to study.



Sample A smaller subset selected from the population.




Key idea: We usually study the sample because studying the entire population is too difficult, expensive, or impossible.

title: STA258
text:
Example: Population and Sample

Population: All students at the University of Toronto.  Sample: 50 randomly selected students from the University of Toronto.


Main idea:
We study the sample to learn about the population.

Make sure students understand that the sample is not the final target — the population is.

title: STA258
text:
Unit, Observation, Variable

Unit The smallest entity from which data are collected.



Observation The collection of measured values for one unit.



Variable A characteristic that can be measured or recorded.

title: STA258
text:
Example: Unit, Observation, Variable

If we record a student’s age, height, and weight: 


The student is the unit


The full row of recorded values is the observation


Age, height, and weight are variables





Quick check: If a dataset has 100 students and 4 measured characteristics per student, how many observations are there?

title: STA258
text:
Population Unit vs Sample Unit



Population unit One member of the full population.



If the population is all UTM students, one UTM student is a population unit.



Sample unit One member of the selected sample.



If the sample is 80 selected UTM students, one selected student is a sample unit.

title: STA258
text:
Dataset Language








Term
Meaning
Example




Unit
Who or what data are collected from
One student


Observation
Full row of data for one unit
Age, height, weight for one student


Variable
Characteristic being recorded
Age





This table is useful when students first learn how to read datasets.

title: STA258
text:
Parameter vs Statistic



Parameter A numerical quantity that describes a population.



Statistic A numerical quantity that describes a sample.




Memory trick: Parameter → Population   |   Statistic → Sample

title: STA258
text:
Parameter vs Statistic: Examples

Parameters (population-level):


Population mean, \(\mu\)


Population standard deviation, \(\sigma\)


Population proportion, \(p\)



Statistics (sample-level):


Sample mean, \(\bar{x}\)


Sample standard deviation, \(s\)


Sample proportion, \(\hat{p}\)




Students often mix up \(p\) and \(\hat{p}\). Stress that \(p\) is population-level and \(\hat{p}\) is sample-level.

title: STA258
text:
Parameter vs Statistic: Summary Table



Population Quantity
Sample Quantity
Meaning




\(\mu\)
\(\bar{x}\)
Mean


\(\sigma\)
\(s\)
Standard deviation


\(p\)
\(\hat{p}\)
Proportion






We use sample statistics like \(\bar{x}\) or \(\hat{p}\) to estimate population parameters like \(\mu\) or \(p\).

title: STA258
text:
Descriptive vs Inferential Statistics

Descriptive statistics Methods used to analyze, summarize, and represent data.



Inferential statistics Using sample information to make conclusions about a larger population.

title: STA258
text:
Descriptive vs Inferential: Examples


Descriptive

The average mark in a class of 80 students is 72%.


Inferential

Based on 80 students, we estimate the average mark for all STA258 students.

title: STA258
text:
1.2 Types of Data

Quantitative data Data that can be measured numerically.



Examples: height, weight, age, temperature




Question: Does every number automatically mean the variable is quantitative?

title: STA258
text:
Qualitative Data

Qualitative data Data that cannot be measured numerically — instead, it falls into categories.



Examples: favourite colour, eye colour, ice-cream flavour

title: STA258
text:
Quantitative vs Qualitative



Data
Type
Reason




Height
Quantitative
Measured numerically


Number of courses
Quantitative
Counted numerically


Favourite colour
Qualitative
Falls into a category


Student number
Qualitative
The number is only an identifier




The student number example is useful — it shows that numbers are not always quantitative.

title: STA258
text:
Discrete and Continuous Data



Discrete data Quantitative data that are countable.



Examples: number of siblings, number of defects



Continuous data Quantitative data measured on a scale.



Examples: height, time, temperature

title: STA258
text:
Quick Check: Types of Data
Classify each variable:

Number of courses a student is taking
Student’s major
Time spent studying per week
Whether a student lives on campus



Answers:


Quantitative, discrete


Qualitative


Quantitative, continuous


Qualitative

title: STA258
text:
1.3 Introduction to Inferential Statistics

Sample → Statistic → Estimate population parameter


Statistical inference uses sample data to estimate population values and measure uncertainty.
\[
\text{Sample} \rightarrow \text{Statistic} \rightarrow \text{Parameter}
\]

title: STA258
text:
Big Picture

We usually cannot measure the whole population.  So we collect a sample, calculate a statistic, and use it to estimate the unknown parameter.



Important: Because samples vary, inference must always include a measure of uncertainty.

title: STA258
text:
Main Inference Tools

Estimation Using sample data to guess the value of a population parameter.


Confidence intervals Giving a plausible range of values for a population parameter.


Hypothesis testing Checking whether sample data support a specific claim.


Pause before revealing the answers. This is a good check for whether students understand sample vs population and statistic vs parameter.

title: STA258
text:
Chapter 1 Summary

Statistics is the science of learning from data
A sample is used to study a population
Parameters describe populations; statistics describe samples
Data can be quantitative or qualitative
Quantitative data can be discrete or continuous
Inference uses samples to draw population-level conclusions

title: STA258
text:
End of Chapter 1

Change the code so it calculates the sample mean of the vector.
















Solution

title: STA258
text:
Roadmap


Setup and Purpose

2.1 The Statistical Computing Language
2.2 Installing R and RStudio
2.2.3 Positron


Programming Basics

2.3 Programming in R
R as a Calculator
Data Structures
Functions, Histograms, and Packages

title: STA258
text:
2.1 The Statistical Computing Language

R R is a programming language used for data manipulation, statistics, graphics, and reproducible analysis.


R is useful because it can:

perform calculations on numbers, vectors, arrays, and matrices
generate high-quality graphs
use built-in functions and user-contributed packages
save analysis steps in scripts that can be reused later

title: STA258
text:
Why Use R?



Point-and-click software Often easier at first, but steps may be harder to document and repeat.



R scripts Each step is written down, so the analysis is easier to check, repeat, and modify.




Main idea: R makes statistical work reproducible, transparent, and easier to automate.

title: STA258
text:
2.2 Installing R and RStudio
Before working with R code, students need two things:



R The programming language that performs the calculations.



RStudio The interface used to write scripts, run code, view files, and inspect output.




Think of R as the engine, and RStudio as the dashboard used to control it.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R

Step 1 Go to the official R project website: cran.r-project.org



Choose your operating system:


Windows: Download R for Windows → base → download the installer


macOS: Download R for macOS → choose the correct .pkg file


Linux: Follow the distribution-specific instructions




After installation, R can be accessed from a terminal or through RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
Installing R: Teaching Note

Important: Students should install R before installing RStudio, because RStudio needs an R installation to run code.



The ebook screenshot shows the CRAN page where students choose their operating system and download the correct installer.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.2 Installing RStudio

RStudio RStudio is an integrated development environment designed for working with R.


It provides:

a script editor
an R console
an environment and history viewer
a file browser
plots, packages, help, viewer, and presentation panes

title: STA258
text:
RStudio Workflow

Basic workflow:


Open RStudio.


Create a new script.


Write code in the Source pane.


Run the code.


Read the output in the Console or Plots pane.





Shortcut: Use Cmd + Enter (Mac) or Ctrl + Enter (Windows) to run the current line of code.

title: STA258
text:
The Four Main RStudio Panes







Pane
Purpose




Source
Write and edit scripts or Quarto files


Console
Run commands and view immediate output


Environment / History
View saved objects and past commands


Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer
Manage files, plots, packages, and documentation





The ebook shows the RStudio home screen and the layout after opening a new script.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.3 Positron

Positron Positron is another desktop environment from Posit for data science and statistical computing.


For this course, students mainly need RStudio.


Positron may be useful in future workflows, but it is not required for STA258.

title: STA258
section: 2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R
text:
2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R
R is especially useful for statistics because it is designed for:

data analysis
statistical calculations
visualization
reproducible reporting



Course goal: Students do not need to become software engineers. They need enough R to calculate, summarize, visualize, and report data clearly.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.1 R as a Powerful Calculator

R can be used like a calculator. It supports arithmetic, exponentiation, logarithms, trigonometric functions, and summary calculations.





Operation
R Code
Result




Addition
2 + 3
5


Subtraction
4 - 1
3


Multiplication
6 * 2
12


Division
8 / 2
4


Exponentiation
5^2
25

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Basic Arithmetic
Edit the numbers and run the code.

title: STA258
text:
More Arithmetic Operators



Operation
R Code
Meaning




Modulo
9 %% 4
Remainder after division


Integer division
9 %/% 4
Whole-number part of division

title: STA258
text:
Vector Operations

Vector A vector is a one-dimensional collection of values of the same type.












Operations on vectors are performed element by element.

title: STA258
text:
Math Functions in R
R has many built-in mathematical functions.



Function
Meaning
Example




sqrt()
Square root
sqrt(16)


log()
Natural log
log(10)


log10()
Log base 10
log10(100)


exp()
Exponential function
exp(1)


abs()
Absolute value
abs(-5)


round()
Round to decimal places
round(3.1415, 2)

title: STA258
text: Try It: Math Functions

title: STA258
text:
Summary Functions
Common functions for summarizing data:



Function
Meaning




mean(x)
Average


sum(x)
Total


min(x)
Minimum


max(x)
Maximum


sd(x)
Standard deviation


var(x)
Variance

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Summary Functions
Change the values in x, then rerun the code.

title: STA258
text:
Quick Practice: Calculate the Mean
Fill in the blank to calculate the mean.
















Solution


mean(x)

title: STA258
text:
2.3.2 Basic R Data Structures
The primary data structures introduced in the ebook are:



Vectors
Lists
Matrices



Arrays
Factors
Data frames




Main idea: Different data structures organize information in different ways.

title: STA258
text:
Vectors

Vector A one-dimensional sequence of values of the same type.












Vectors are the basic building blocks for many R operations.

title: STA258
text:
Lists

List A flexible structure that can store elements of different types and sizes.

title: STA258
text:
Matrices

Matrix A two-dimensional data structure with rows and columns. All elements must be the same type.

title: STA258
text:
Matrix Operations










The operator %*% is used for matrix multiplication. t() transposes a matrix.

title: STA258
text:
Arrays

Array A multi-dimensional structure that can have more than two dimensions.

title: STA258
text:
Factors

Factor A data structure used to represent categorical variables.












Factors are useful for categorical data such as gender, education level, or survey responses.

title: STA258
text:
Data Frames

Data frame A table-like structure where columns are variables and rows are observations.

title: STA258
text:
Data Structure Summary



Structure
Shape
Best Used For




Vector
One dimension
Values of the same type


List
Flexible
Mixed types or grouped information


Matrix
Two dimensions
Same-type numerical tables


Array
Multiple dimensions
Higher-dimensional data


Factor
One dimension
Categorical variables


Data frame
Rows and columns
Real datasets

title: STA258
text:
2.3.3 Using Basic Functions in R
R has many built-in functions that help calculate, transform, and summarize data.

title: STA258
text:
Function Logic

General form Most R functions use the structure function_name(input).



For example, mean(x) tells R to apply the function mean to the object x.



Common mistake: Forgetting brackets, commas, or quotation marks will cause R to return an error.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.4 Make a Histogram Using RStudio
The ebook uses the built-in faithful dataset to demonstrate a histogram.

Histogram A graph that shows the distribution of a numerical variable by grouping values into intervals called bins.

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Create a Histogram
Run the code to create a histogram of waiting times in the faithful dataset.

title: STA258
text:
Histogram Breaks and Counts
The ebook also shows how to access the numerical values behind a histogram.










breaks show the interval endpoints. counts show how many observations fall in each interval.

title: STA258
text:
Quick Practice: Histogram Variable
Change the code so it creates a histogram of eruptions instead of waiting.
















Solution


hist(faithful$eruptions)

title: STA258
text:
2.3.5 Installing and Loading Packages

Package A package is a collection of extra functions, data, and tools that extend what R can do.



Install a package once:
install.packages("ggplot2")
Load it every new R session:
library(ggplot2)

title: STA258
text:
Package Reminder

Important: You install a package only once, but you must load it with library() every time you restart RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
Chapter 2 Summary

R is used for statistics, graphics, data manipulation, and reproducible analysis
RStudio is the main interface for writing and running R code
R can be used as a calculator for arithmetic and mathematical functions
Vectors, lists, matrices, arrays, factors, and data frames each store data differently
Functions take inputs and produce outputs using the form function_name(input)
Histograms display the distribution of a numerical variable
Packages extend R’s functionality and must be loaded each session with library()

title: STA258
text:
End of Chapter 2
